we have `[⟪pbar.rotR' ..., w⟫, etc.]`, which are `⟪∂θ₁ rotprojRM ... S, w⟫`, etc. 

Thus, `⟪(rotprojRMS') z, w⟫ = z₁ ⟪∂θ₁ rotprojRMS' ... S, w⟫ + ...`, because:
`⟪(rotprojRMS') z, w⟫ = ⟪ [∂θ₁ ..., ∂θ₂ ..., ∂α ...] • z, w⟫ = z₁ ⟪∂θ₁ ..., w⟫ + ...`. 

But `[⟪∂θ₁ ..., w⟫, etc.] • z = z₁ ⟪∂θ₁ ..., w⟫ + ...`. 

So `⟪(rotprojRMS') z, w⟫ = [⟪∂θ₁ ..., w⟫, etc.] • z`. 

But `rotproj_inner' pbar S w` is defined to be `[⟪∂θ₁ ..., w⟫, etc.] • z`. 

Thus, they are indeed equal. 

Therefore, the statement is true because we have correctly defined `rotproj_inner'` as `[⟪∂θ₁ ..., w⟫, etc.]`. 

But in Lean, we don’t have `rotprojRMS'` defined, so perhaps this is not directly usable.

    -- Compose with the inner product to get the derivative of `rotproj_inner`

      have h₂₄ : HasFDerivAt (fun (y : ℝ²) => ⟪y, w⟫) (ContinuousLinearMap.tsum (Pi.basis (fun _ : Fin 2 => ℝ) ℝ) |>.toContinuousLinearMap) (rotprojRM (pbar.innerParams.ofLp 1) (pbar.innerParams.ofLp 2) (pbar.innerParams.ofLp 0) S) := by

        apply hasFDerivAt_inner_right
        <;> continuity
      -- Combine the derivatives using the chain rule
      have h₂₅ : HasFDerivAt (fun x : ℝ³ => ⟪rotprojRM (x.ofLp 1) (x.ofLp 2) (x.ofLp 0) S, w⟫) (ContinuousLinearMap.tsum (Pi.basis (fun _ : Fin 2 => ℝ) ℝ) |>.toContinuousLinearMap ∘L (rotprojRM' S)) pbar.innerParams := by
        have h₂₅₁ : HasFDerivAt (fun x : ℝ³ => (rotprojRM (x.ofLp 1) (x.ofLp 2) (x.ofLp 0)) S) (rotprojRM' S) pbar.innerParams := h₂₃
        have h₂₅₂ : HasFDerivAt (fun (y : ℝ²) => ⟪y, w⟫) (ContinuousLinearMap.tsum (Pi.basis (fun _ : Fin 2 => ℝ) ℝ) |>.toContinuousLinearMap) (rotprojRM (pbar.innerParams.ofLp 1) (pbar.innerParams.ofLp 2) (pbar.innerParams.ofLp 0) S) := h₂₄
        -- Use the chain rule to combine the derivatives
        have h₂₅₃ : HasFDerivAt (fun x : ℝ³ => ⟪rotprojRM (x.ofLp 1) (x.ofLp 2) (x.ofLp 0) S, w⟫) (ContinuousLinearMap.tsum (Pi.basis (fun _ : Fin 2 => ℝ) ℝ) |>.toContinuousLinearMap ∘L (rotprojRM' S)) pbar.innerParams := by

        rw [h₂₆] at h₂₅

  -- Use the fact that `rotproj_inner` is the same as the inner product
  have h₃ : HasFDerivAt (fun x : ℝ³ => ⟪rotprojRM (x.ofLp 1) (x.ofLp 2) (x.ofLp 0) S, w⟫) (rotproj_inner' pbar S w) pbar.innerParams := h₂
  -- Relate the `rotproj_inner` function to the inner product
  have h₄ : rotproj_inner S w = (fun x : ℝ³ => ⟪rotprojRM (x.ofLp 1) (x.ofLp 2) (x.ofLp 0) S, w⟫) := by

    exact h₃
  -- Conclude the proof
